content:
Character: Julian Reyes
Perception: Julian is stocky, dark-eyed, those tattooed forearms always crossed like he's bracing for something. He's not conventionally handsome but there's a warmth in his face when he talks about his grandmother that makes everything else disappear. He's clearly skilled at what he does — passionate about food in a way that reminds me of Nina with her violin, that total devotion to craft. He's guarded, humble, probably too hard on himself. We had one long rooftop conversation in January and he told me about leaving Chicago, about his grandmother's recipes, about guilt. I told him about Dolores. I think he sees me as someone slightly out of his reach, which breaks my heart because he doesn't see how remarkable he is. He's becoming something important to me that I don't have a name for yet. I know he sends money home, that his father was injured, that he cooks for the building like it's a form of prayer.
summary:
Character: Julian Reyes
Perception: Julian is stocky, dark-eyed, those tattooed forearms always crossed like he's bracing for something. He's not conventionally handsome but there's a warmth in his face when he talks about his grandmother that makes everything else disappear. He's clearly skilled at what he does — passionate about food in a way that reminds me of Nina with her violin, that total devotion to craft. He's guarded, humble, probably too hard on himself. We had one long rooftop conversation in January and he told me about leaving Chicago, about his grandmother's recipes, about guilt. I told him about Dolores. I think he sees me as someone slightly out of his reach, which breaks my heart because he doesn't see how remarkable he is. He's becoming something important to me that I don't have a name for yet. I know he sends money home, that his father was injured, that he cooks for the building like it's a form of prayer.
